items.q: What does a correlation of -0.8 indicate?
items.misconception: A weak relationship (because it is negative).
items.why: Students treat the negative sign as 'weak'. The sign shows DIRECTION (as one variable goes up, the other goes down); the absolute value shows STRENGTH. -0.8 is strong.

items.q: What is the difference between reliability and validity?
items.correct: Reliability is consistency; validity is whether it measures what it claims to.
items.misconception: They are essentially the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Reliability is for quantitative, validity for qualitative.
items.why: Students treat these as synonyms. A bathroom scale that adds 5kg every time is reliable (consistent) but not valid (wrong reading). You can be reliable without being valid.

items.q: In Milgram's study, what percentage of participants gave the maximum 450V shock?
items.why: Students remember 'most' obeyed and assume all did. Actually 65% went to 450V; 35% defied. This nuance matters because it shows obedience is strong but not absolute.

items.q: Which test fits ordinal data with unrelated groups testing for difference?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Mann-Whitney. “Sign test” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What is the conventional significance level in psychology?
items.why: Correct answer: p < 0.05. “p < 0.1” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Type I error rate at the conventional p < 0.05 threshold is what percent?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
